title: Steph Ortiz -Operations Interview | Engagement Manager (Account Management)
meeting_title: Steph Ortiz -Operations Interview | Engagement Manager (Account Management)

transcript.text: How are you?
This meeting is being recorded.
My voice sounds a little, sounds a little gross.
I've been sick for a few days, but I'm feeling better.
Oh, I'm, I've been in the same boat.
I'm just on the tail end of like a two week cold.
And so I'm like a little nasally, still drinking tea.
So I hear you.
I know, the length is so frustrating.
Like it's usually just one or two days for me, but when it lingers, it's just.
Oh my gosh.
Yeah, totally.
And I feel like everyone, I live in a mountain town just north of Vancouver.
Everyone I've talked to here has been like same exact boat, two weeks of like just a lingering sickness, but.
See, I have no excuse because I'm in San Francisco, which is not cold at all.
Oh, nice.
Oh, I love San Francisco.
Have you been there for a while?
Yeah.
So we're, we're headquartered out here.
We're a remote company.
Right.
But we have an office out here in SF and I've been there for two years.
So a year and a half before I started working here.
So, yeah, so far so good.
Oh, nice.
Amazing.
I love it out there.
I have some friends out there, so I'll occasionally visit from time to time.
Amazing.
Lovely.
Well, I know we don't have too much time and we'd love to learn a bunch more about you.
So I guess maybe just generally, like, we'd love to hear more about, I guess, how you got to this point and just a little bit more you, like, what your interests are, strengths are.
then I can dig into, like, more of the role-specific questions that I have, but we'd love just to get a picture on, like, who Stephanie is first.
Yeah, for sure.
So I live in a town called Squamish.
Not sure if you've heard of it.
It's just north of Vancouver, halfway between Whistler and Vancouver, essentially.
Lovely mountain town.
I do all the mountain activities out here.
I was in Vancouver for about a decade before that, before moving here.
And currently I'm working as an enterprise customer success manager at a company called Docebo.
They're an LMS system and working with large, complex customers responsible for, like, all the regular CSM type of responsibility.
And then prior to this, I was actually at a smaller scale, much smaller, early stage startup called Notaro.
They're a practice management software for physios, athletic therapists, massage therapists.
It's a software they use essentially to run their practice.
And I was the first customer-facing team member there and built out a small customer-facing team and managed the team overall.
And that role I really loved because I was really able to kind of build processes and build out teams and train people and interview and all of that.
But before that, though, I was working at Moz, so an SEO software company.
They're based out of Seattle.
Yeah.
So I was there for four years.
And that was my essentially my first job in software, actually.
And so I really enjoyed being in the SEO space and being involved in that space related to content.
I was also a content writer.

And so how I got to this point, really reached out to me on LinkedIn directly about this opportunity initially, and then I wasn't too sure what the agency did, and it was like a little bit confused, but then more I did some reading and talked to people internally about it, was like, oh, this is interesting.
It's, you know, back in the SEO space, but kind of in this new era with AI.

And so definitely very interested in exploring it and going back into it, because one, always been fascinated and interested by content and the SEO space in particular, but I have been out of that space for, I would say, four years or so.
So I know things have probably changed a little bit, for sure.
And I hope that answers your question.
I'm not sure if there's anything else that I missed there, but that's what brought me to this point.
And so just more intrigued to learn more about the opportunity and the team members involved, and yeah, that's how I got to this point.
Yeah, amazing, amazing.
I guess generally, right?
Like the space, well, honestly, like the world, but the space itself, SEO, content, AEO, like it's just moving so fast.

And so curious, even though you removed from the SEO world for a bit, like even in your current role, like how is your company or you more specifically like leveraging AI tools and like trying to stay on top of everything that's happened?
Like we'd love to hear more about like anything that you played around with or how you're trying to stay ahead.
Yeah, absolutely.
I feel like our CEO is very much like drilling it into us and being like, we are an AI forward, AI first kind of company.
And so because we're a learning management software, we constantly have to take courses very regularly.
And so some of those are AI focused as well.
So we're learning, we've learned very much like the basics of like agentic, generative AI, as well as incorporating tools, new tools all the time.
And so what that.
looks like, for example, of course, ChatTPT, Gemini for preparing client presentations kind of speeds up my process a little bit, as well as we have our own internal AI tools that we've built.
So all of our internal kind of documentation for processes are all uploaded into various agentic kind of AI tools, and then we use those to pull, say, like reports before a renewal for a customer.

So instead of having to dig across different platforms and across different resources, we can just pull it up using our internal AI tools really, really quickly, which has been saving us a ton of time, especially in, like, a busy client-facing role.
I'm trying to think of what else we've been using.
Of course, note-taking tools.

And then with the internal AI tools that I have obviously not built myself, I'm required to test them out and provide feedback internally to ensure that everything's, you know, accurate and up to quality for the rest of the team as well.
Mm-hmm.
Amazing.
Yeah.

Because most of it sounds like, you know, the company's building like an internal knowledge base of context on the company, how they work, but also like client specific knowledge base as well, where you can just like interact with all of these context documents and like get questions answered, feedback, help create materials.
Exactly.
Versus having to waste time digging through every kind of platform we use internally.
It's all kind of centralized into this one AI localized place.
Amazing.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's cool.
Is there anything like, actually, no, we could stop it.
It's really cool.
There's just so much that we're doing internally as well.
Like context is like the core of what matters.
But then like all of our workflows, everything we do on the content side are powered by like really powerful technology.
so being able to communicate that is important.
Just like be like naturally interested as well.
So it's good that you have that and have like some experience and it's all learnable, but Yeah.
There is like a level of it's changing so quickly, like the testing and iteration and like you giving the product feedback sparked it in my brain.
Like that is most important.
Like our CEO and CTO, they're still testing each new tool that comes out and saying like, okay, how is it better?
Where can we use it?
How can we update our models and our workflows to have results with it?
And so that mindset is super important.
Wonderful.
Yeah, that's great to hear.
Great to hear.
Cool.
On the like client and account management side, I heard you mentioning like handling renewals and expansions.
I guess it might look different in the learning management space like where you're currently at.

But do you have an example of when you kind of just developed, pitched a strategy and then like the end to end execution of like, okay, here's the idea, here's the pitch, getting the client buy-in and then.
Now we have to execute on it and, like, report against it.
So, like, from strategy down to, like, actual execution and, like, reporting.
Do you have, like, a good example that you could call to?
Yeah, that's a really good question.
It's hard.
It is a hard question, yeah.
I'm to think of, like, even just recent interactions that I've had.
And I have more specific questions as well, if it's more helpful.
And then we could, like, get to the bigger one, too.
Yeah.
I guess overall, this is kind of, like, functionality, but it does tie into strategy.
We recently onboarded a really big client that I've taken on.
And their level of knowledge of the platform and LMS systems in general are really varied.
And so what I noticed when I went into their platform, essentially, was it was set up in an...
It wasn't set up optimally.
And so I had to think, okay...
Achieving their goals.
the start of every client interaction, when I'm meeting a new account or client, I always talk about goals and objectives and what our long-term strategy will hopefully look like and meet in between.
And I basically had to sit them down and say, hey, this is going to interfere with our goals.
This is going to get in the way.
Definitely, it's going to cause these type of problems, even in the short to medium term, but definitely in the long term of achieving our wider range goals and objectives for the year.

I really needed to get their buy-in to, I had to really explain the why of like, okay, I know this is going to be time consuming for you to go back into the platform, get rid of all of this that you've already poured so much hard work into and start from scratch, essentially, which is like a really hard pill to swallow when you're really busy on your end as well.
And so I tried to explain it in a way that was like created, created an action plan for them, essentially, like, here are the steps, what I did for you was create a very clear action plan.
And like a timeline and explaining the why and the impact of each, and then tying those to their like longer term objectives and goals and explaining, if you do this, we can achieve this as planned.
If we don't do this, we are going to reach this X, Y, and Z type of blockers.
And then this is going to definitely be delayed.
And we might not even achieve this one result or outcome that we're looking to achieve.
It's very like general, but I had to really sit them down and explain it because they were really pushing back on wanting to do this.

But having that conversation and showing them the action plan that I created and like poured my hard work and time into and really just spelling it out and getting their buy-in that way was, you know, some of the team members were a little bit like, oh, okay, fine.
But in the end, we were able to all get on board and align on what needed to be done.
And I was, what I kind of, what I did in that situation was say, hey, I know we only meet monthly.
Let's meet bi-weekly.
Let's do regular check-ins to see where we're at and see what pain points you're kind of running into.
And then hopefully I can help kind of minimize those as we continue.
And so it was a lot of like, I was like, I don't know if they're going to get on board with this.
But in the end, we were able to get everyone aligned and move forward.
So that was a tough one for sure.
Yeah, I mean, those can be so hard, but just so many positive things jumped at them ahead.
Like as you were explaining that, especially as it relates to like the role and the strategy sprints and like the eight-week onboarding program.
Like one thing that I don't even know if we've struggled with, maybe, yeah, maybe to be candid, that we've struggled with is like there's so many different types of clients.
And we have an idea right now that we filter for it during sales of like who's a great client to work with.
And why?
Well, once you get into the post-sales, eight-week sprints, we need to.
Figure out how we can add the most value.
You need to constantly realign the client's goals.
One, deeply understand their goals and the why behind everything that they're doing.
And then tie it to all the work that we're doing and be super clear about how we're actually moving the needle on those things and how we're making progress.
And so that was amazing to hear that you already did proactively.
Because that's a super important part.
And it's only going to become more important moving forward as our platform progresses.
Because more of it's going to happen within the platform, but then the setup becomes more key.

And so there will always be that personal engagement manager touch and need for understanding the strategy, setting it, really understand the client's goals, and making sure everything is set up correctly.
And then on like the activities and actions, like the execution piece, like it's not just, hey, we're doing these five.
proved.
Thank
Things for you every week.
It's like, this is why we're doing these things.
This is where we're going.
And this is how we're getting there.
Yes, we did these things.
And this is like amazing progress that we made.
But like, this is where we're going.
And so this is what we're doing next as well.
And it's like, that's another level of like proactivity.
That's like, definitely required and hard.
Yeah, definitely.
Always explaining the why behind we're doing things is a way to, I think, get through to clients in tough situations.
And that's always been my approach.
Yeah, because ultimately, they come to us to be like the thought partner and leader in the space and to drive the engagement.
So like, there's so many different layers there.
But that was really good.
I think maybe like with the example you gave already, like, what were the metrics that you're trying that you were like managing to or like the goals that you were managing to?
And then like, how would you communicate that to the clients or like reporting in place?
And then like, how did you come up with those metrics?
Because it's of standard practice across your entire book.
A business or custom for each client.
Oh, right.
You did mention the reporting piece.
I forgot to address that.
Sorry.
So you're asking, are there reporting metrics that we measure for each client?
And then also, are there certain more custom ones for different events?
You can ground it in the example that you gave of the client that you already talked about.
What were the metrics and goals you were managing to there?
And then how were you actually managing to them?
Is it like, you mentioned now you're meeting bi-weekly instead of monthly.
Is there a reporting structure in place?
Or how do you communicate with the client?
Things like that.
Right.
Of course.

So one thing they were definitely trying to measure was course completions because they're essentially just like sharing out courses internally to train up their internal employees on various type of topics.
And so because of their current setup, it was creating issues with actually being able to efficiently push those courses to their different groups in an efficient way.
And so because of...
The V, I'll try to explain this in a way that makes sense, but it's the way they were doing it was going to truly mess up their reporting from the start.
How they initially set it up was they weren't grouping and like segmenting their groups for reporting purposes.
And so when they did that, I was showing them your reporting.
This is an example report of the way things were previously set up.
If you're trying to measure, say, per department in a company, things weren't segmented in a way where you could actually pull those metrics.
It was all grouped together.
And so pulling that, yeah, it was messy.
And then being able to pull a separate report of like, this is what it could look like and what it should look like afterwards.

If you want an accurate, more granular reporting that you can actually do something with and actually get accurate numbers on how each department is completing like their courses and at what time and are they missing any targets.
I was able to pull an example report from a test account to show the accurate setup and then able to present that to them.
We're now at the stage where we haven't actually been able to pull a ton of those reports, but we should be at that stage now where they can pull those themselves.
And we set it up as like a reoccurring kind of monthly report now that's automatic.

But in terms of like how we measure success as a customer success manager, it really depends on a few things like engagement, how often they're, you know, in the platform and then looking at how many users they actually have registered in the platform and how many we have allocated to them.

Are they like, for example, I think this account had 2000 seats, for example, in their platform at the time when they first started, they only had like maybe a hundred being used, which is going to create an issue long-term if that stays the case.
So going over as we're continuously meeting, I'm like, oh, great.
Lyft, they're one of my accounts.
I took them this morning.
Oh, no way.
Nice.
Yeah, so great account.
A bit complicated because the person who was managing them previously had left.
And so the renewal was coming up in three months.
And it was, I don't like to start a renewal process just three months out the gate.
I'm like, oh, we need like at least six months to kind of plan for that.
And so I had to look at all the metrics, look at where they were at in terms of usage.
And then noticed some things that were potentially concerning and looped in the appropriate team members to say, hey, do we have any more context on Lyft?
I'm new to this account.
The renewal's coming up super soon.
And we work very closely with account managers.
we have our CSMs and then the account managers in our company, and we work as like a pair.
So working really closely with them to talk about the commercial aspects and say, hey, this is all the research I was able to pull up.
Looks like they're good to renew.
What do we think here?
They were able to say, like, they noticed, yeah, they're not quite using the required amount of seats.
So there might be a bit of a downgrade there.
And I was like, oh, that's unfortunate.
Let's see.
Let's get them on a call to show them our actually our new AI tools that we've incorporated because it looks like they're not actually maximizing on those.
And some of them are free and some of them are paid.
And so we're able to arrange a call, get the whole lift, like most of the executive team on the learning team and some of their users as well on this call.
And I demoed the platform and showed them the areas that they weren't using, which they could benefit and save a ton of time building content using our AI tools and then our paid AI tools as well.
And they were blown away.
They were like, oh, wow, we didn't know about these because our customer success manager has been kind of non-existent.
We've just haven't had someone on our account for the last few months.
And we are very interested in these included AI tools, but also the paid.
And so we were able to upsell there and show them all the time that they could save.
in creating learning content.
And it was, it was a, yeah, a quick, but good win in the end.
Nice.
I love that.
I would be lying if I said, you know, we have six months ahead of every renewal to be prepared.
Honestly, same.
It doesn't always work out that way.
Yeah.
But that's like the standard the company tries to set.
And sometimes it's honestly, things happen internally all the time.
And sometimes it's, you get a new account and you're like, oh, we have two months or a month.
Yeah.
You didn't make it work.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No, it's the right way to think about it though.
And then like with the sprints, with the strategy sprints, it's like the first eight weeks are essentially, you already know you're basically in renewal mode.
It's like, let's prove out our value now.
So that phase two is a no brainer, like moving on to phase two.
Yeah.
Because of that mutual opt-in opt-out at the end of the eight weeks.
And so it's always like a good mindset to be in.
It's kind of like to always be selling mindset as you're delivering value as well.
So it's cool that you work closely with the account manager and have, and so you've got those.
Skills as well.
guess that was a bunch of my questions.
I I could always ask more questions, but I wanted to leave at least a few minutes in case you had any questions or wanted to learn a bit more, like dig in anywhere I'm happy to answer.
Yeah, for sure.
I feel like because I've been out of the SEO space for quite a while, I'm actually, truthfully, I'm still learning like how AI fits into that and how the agency, I guess, what the process is.
And if you could just paint like more of a holistic picture on how it works with our customers when it comes to the AI piece and how that fits in with SEO.
Yeah.
be awesome.
I'm sending you a link, right?
Sure.
Yeah, that would be great.
We just launched our own AI visibility platform last week, which is exciting.
I just sent in a Zoom chat.
Cool.
Thank you.
And so the question.
These model companies are also have bots, training bots, indexing bots, et cetera, that will ping your content and give you signals on like, is my content being used to train these models?
Because ultimately, right, like what you can control, the experience you can control for your business is your website.
You can change pages on your website.
You can update your brand on the website.
You have control over this asset, right?
This asset, your website, speaks to and influences the way everybody views your business.
Again, audience and real people, that's these models, AI models, and that's these training bots that come to your website for content.
And so we're optimizing this website experience for our clients.
And the way to do that, what's most controllable on the website are pages on the website and content on the website.
And so that's like the quick and dirty of how we're prioritizing.
And then like what we're seeing a lot from clients that they want to see is tracking custom prompts.
So like what are the questions?
That your audience is asking, right?
And how, if and how are we answering that in the content that we're creating?
So when you, in traditional SEO, when you create an article, right?
You get signals.
Is it the article indexed?
Is the article getting impressions?
Is it getting clicks?
Cool, clicks converting.
Now on the AO side, the signals that you can ingest and use to influence the strategy are, is my brand visible?
Am I coming up at all, right?
What are the prompts, the prompts that I'm tracking, the questions that people are asking?
Is my brand getting mentioned?
So like when someone's searching for what is left, is my brand being mentioned?

And then is my brand being cited, citations, which are like the most controllable piece and like the most important is like, is the piece of content I'm creating being linked and cited in the answer and influencing the answer?
And so all of that are like...
Data points that we now track and own in our visibility tool.
what a bunch of other tools in the market do it too.
But we do it, in my opinion, better because tracking is one thing, but being able to incorporate that into, okay, we're cited here.
This is the piece of content that we wrote.
These are the actual words that we use that were cited.
Here's what we're doing to improve your strategy based on that.
Now we're going to write more content in this content type.
We're going to speak more about this and take this angle, and we're going to place that bet down to see is this working or not.
And so it's like a content operating system that we're training versus just blogs on the website, right?
Got it.
Okay.
Thank you.

That helps answer that question because I felt like I know I've been out of this space for a little while and just trying to connect those dots in terms of how it all works together and in this kind of way for this agency.
So thank you.
It's very different.
It's like, what do we do?
We post blogs on the website versus the explanation I just gave like it's.
I'm sure there's way more to it.
Yeah, it's truly meaningful, right?
Because, yeah, to do it at scale, you need super powerful tech.
And there's a bunch of other stuff that we're doing that's really, really cool, too.
But, yeah, hopefully that helped.
That does help a lot.
Just out of curiosity, you mentioned competitors.
Who are your top competitors these days?
Yeah, so it's kind of like two on the content front and workflow.
So optimization front for content, it's Aerops is one.
And Graphite is a more boutique, high-ticket one.
Those are two that were constantly monitoring.
then on the AEO front, like Profound is a visibility tool.
There's like 200 other tools now that just like people are dropping.
Oh, wow.
Oh, yeah.
But ours is fundamentally different in a bunch of really, really good ways.
Okay, cool.
How are you doing for a time?
I just want to be really mindful of time today.
You're okay?
I can go over by a few minutes if you're okay with
Okay.
And just out of curiosity, what's the work culture like?
I actually haven't had a chance to ask this question to William.
had a lot to cover.
And so how would you describe the work culture at GrowthX?
Yeah.
I don't know why I'm smiling, honestly.
For me, I mean, I love it.
But I guess the best way to put it is whatever you want is there if you want it, right?

Like if you want more and growth and to do more within the company, if you just demonstrate value, then it's there for you because we're still so small and so nimble and we're still building a bunch of processes and we're still learning as we go.
Like we're very open to initiative and proactivity and just like people who are performing very well.
So that to me is like super important and amazing.
It's like pure, like no politics, like let's all just.
Get to the truth and the best answer here.
And let's see how we can do it most efficiently using a bunch of tools and whatever else.
So that's what I love about it.
I will say it's not like easy work, right?
Because so much stuff has changed.
not like there's a playbook for everything that we do.
There's fundamentals that you follow.
And now we're building out training for this role specifically and like context on the company that helps people get up to speed more, which is great.
But everything's changing so fast.
It's like we're constantly just, I mean, I was just on a client meeting now.
It's like I'm testing and demoing something that I didn't, that I've never done before.
And like, you just got to kind of learn and prep yourself and all of that.
And so it's definitely still like a scrappy environment.
But yeah, but it's amazing.
Like everyone's super talented and, you know, the exposure is pretty insane.
Like the companies that we get and like you're, there's nothing.
noticed.
Like it's just you managing the relationship.
Like it's pretty amazing.
Yeah.
Okay.
Cool.

Yeah, I think there's one thing that I miss is working kind of at like a scrappier type of environment where I'm very much kind of coming from the mindset of like I enjoy building processes when they're not there and kind of if there's the opportunity to do so, I love that stuff personally.
And I think I may have, I don't know if I told William that or not, but if there's the opportunity to do that when it's needed, I love being a part of the building stages.
So that's amazing.
Yeah.
We're actively doing that for the sprints right now.
Yeah.
Cool.
Okay.
I guess, do you think there's any travel required for this role?
might be a William question, but I never got to, because with my job, I have to travel all the time and I'm very indifferent.
Like I could travel like not, but just curious if you know if there's any travel required.
Not required.

What we usually do, honestly, for the sake of like new hires is just get them here for a week in person because learning from the team here and Marcel being able to kind of like meet with everyone and present a bunch.
Stuff on the business is like super valuable.
Everyone who's done it has said it's super valuable.
Like in San Francisco.
Yeah.
That's what you're saying.
I'll put you up.
And then outside of that, no, there's nothing required.
But like as an engagement manager, if you have a bunch of clients that are based in SF and there's a conference and you want to come out, like we're super open to that.
Yeah.
Okay.
Cool.
Okay.
We did cover a lot.
Yeah.
think we've covered most of my things just in our conversation today.
So let's see.
Yeah.
Sorry.
I just have like a list of questions that I want to make sure you cover.
I do too.
I think those are most of my questions for today.
We actually covered a lot in our conversation.
So I appreciate all the information you gave me and painting that picture of how it all works together.
I don't know if there's anything I can provide any clarity on or anything that you may have forgotten to ask me that you want to get a chance to.
I'm sure if there's anything that we forgot to chat up at.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interview Stephanie Ortiz for the Engagement Manager (Account Management) role.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=66.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Strong Fit:** Stephanie Ortiz's experience aligns with the role, including building processes at a startup and proactively managing renewals (e.g., Lyft) and complex client strategy (e.g., a new LMS client).](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=449.0)
  - [**AI-Forward Mindset:** Current role at Docebo requires testing internal AI tools and using generative AI (ChatGPT, Gemini) for client presentations, demonstrating a practical, hands-on approach.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=250.0)
  - [**Strategic Alignment:** Ortiz is drawn to the role as a return to the SEO space, now with an AI focus, and is motivated by the opportunity to build processes in a "scrappy" startup environment.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=181.0)
  - [**GrowthX's AEO Model:** The agency's core strategy is optimizing client websites for AI models, tracking metrics like brand citations and custom prompt answers to build a "content operating system."](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1336.0)

## Topics

### Candidate Background & Motivation

  - [**Current Role:** Enterprise CSM at Docebo (LMS).](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=105.0)
  - [**Past Experience:**](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=105.0)
      - [Built the first customer-facing team and processes at Notaro (practice management software).](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=125.0)
      - [Spent four years at SEO software company Moz.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=158.0)
  - [**Motivation:** Drawn to the role as a return to the SEO space, now with an AI focus.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=181.0)

### AI Experience & Mindset

  - [**Docebo Context:** The company is "AI-first," requiring regular training and hands-on tool use.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=250.0)
  - [**Tool Usage:**](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=250.0)
      - [**Generative AI (ChatGPT, Gemini):** Speeds up client presentation prep.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=299.0)
      - [**Internal Agentic AI:** Centralizes documentation for quick reporting (e.g., renewal reports), saving time.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=300.0)
  - [**Key Insight:** Ortiz actively tests internal tools and provides feedback on accuracy and quality—a mindset George Haikal noted as critical for GrowthX's rapid iteration.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=340.0)

### Strategic Client Management Examples

  - [**Case 1: New LMS Client (Proactive Strategy)**](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=513.0)
      - [**Problem:** A new client's platform setup was suboptimal, jeopardizing long-term goals.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=513.0)
      - [**Action:** Ortiz secured buy-in for a full rebuild by explaining the "why" and its impact on objectives.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=600.0)
      - [**Method:** Presented a clear action plan, timeline, and a "before/after" report to demonstrate the value of accurate data segmentation.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=629.0)
      - [**Outcome:** Client aligned on the rebuild; Ortiz increased check-ins to bi-weekly to support the transition.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=691.0)
  - [**Case 2: Lyft Renewal (Turnaround & Upsell)**](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1140.0)
      - [**Problem:** Inherited the Lyft account with a renewal in three months and low seat utilization (100/2000 seats).](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1147.0)
      - [**Action:** Identified the risk → partnered with the Account Manager → demoed new AI tools to Lyft's executive team.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1170.0)
      - [**Outcome:** Lyft was impressed by the time-saving potential → renewed and purchased a paid AI upsell.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1212.0)

### GrowthX's AI-SEO (AEO) Model

  - [**Core Strategy:** Optimize client websites for AI models, not just human users.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1511.0)
  - [**Key Metrics Tracked:**](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1579.0)
      - [**Visibility:** Is the brand appearing in AI-generated answers?](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1579.0)
      - [**Citations:** Is client content being linked and cited as a source? (Most controllable & important)](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1594.0)
      - [**Prompt Tracking:** Are we answering the specific questions users ask?](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1551.0)
  - [**Goal:** Use these signals to build a "content operating system" that informs strategy and drives results, moving beyond simple blog posts.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1627.0)
  - [**Competitors:** Ahrefs, Graphite (content); Profound (AEO visibility).](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1693.0)

### Company Culture & Logistics

  - [**Culture:** A "scrappy," nimble environment that rewards initiative and performance.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1742.0)
  - [**Travel:** Not required.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1892.0)
      - [**Onsite Option:** New hires can visit the SF office for one week for onboarding.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1892.0)
      - [**Client Visits:** Optional for engagement managers to attend conferences with local clients.](https://fathom.video/share/prTFhAc491Qa6donJdyY_yr465WrAAsh?tab=summary&timestamp=1892.0)
